results.possibleIdentity: Hans Noe (1928–2025) was a distinguished architect and sculptor based in Garrison, New York, who was a significant collaborator and associate of the prominent sculptor Tony Smith during the 1950s, 60s, and 70s. Born in Czernowitz, Austro-Hungary, Noe moved to the United States and became deeply involved in the technical and mathematical aspects of minimalist and modular sculpture. He is particularly noted for assisting Tony Smith with the complex numerical calculations and diagrams required for Smith's modular systems, which were based on tetrahedra and octahedra. This collaborative work was central to pieces such as 'Bat Cave,' which was featured in the 1971 Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) exhibition at the Los Angeles County Museum of Art (LACMA). As an architect, Noe is recognized for designing the studio of Abstract Expressionist painter Elaine de Kooning in East Hampton in 1968. He was also a lender of Tony Smith's works to major institutions like the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) for retrospectives. His career bridged the gap between architecture, engineering, and fine art, aligning with the interdisciplinary goals of organizations like E.A.T.
